content.text: When Mob Programming Is Worth the Cost

Mob programming — the whole team working on one thing at one machine — looks inefficient on a work-items-per-hour basis. It pays off specifically when a task has high coordination cost, high error cost, and distributed expertise requirements. Agent feature development often meets all three criteria.

An agent feature integrates a system prompt, tool schemas, tool implementations, an eval suite, a risk register, and a deployment pipeline. Each of these artefacts has a different expert. Mob programming is the only practice that gives all experts simultaneous visibility over the whole system.
High coordination cost
: the system prompt, tool schemas, eval suite, and deployment pipeline must be consistent with each other. Getting that consistency through async handoffs is slow and error-prone.
High error cost
: a safety violation in an agent feature discovered in production is far more expensive than the same discovery during the mob session.
Distributed expertise
: no single engineer has deep expertise in prompt design, tool-use schema validation, eval methodology, and risk assessment simultaneously.
The Five Mob Roles for Agent Development

attrs.alt: Mob programming roles: Driver types only what the mob says; Navigator directs driver; Spec Guardian reads outputs against must-do/must-not-do; Eval Watcher monitors eval runs; Adversarial Tester generates hostile inputs; Risk Tracker updates risk register

content.text: Role
Primary tool
Rotation frequency
Driver
Keyboard — implements what Navigator dictates
Every 3rd cycle (45 min)
Navigator
Behaviour spec — gives exact instructions to Driver
Every cycle (15 min)
Spec Guardian
Behaviour spec + model output
Every cycle (15 min)
Eval Watcher
Eval runner output in terminal
Every cycle (15 min)
Adversarial Tester
Hostile input generator
Every cycle (15 min)
Risk Tracker
Risk register
Every cycle (15 min)
The Navigator Role: Directing at the Right Level

The Navigator's discipline is giving instructions at the correct abstraction level. Too high ('make the tool safer') and the Driver cannot act on it. Too low ('type a space before the colon on line 3') and the Driver is just a typist.
Right level
: 'Add a constraint after the role definition: Do not make claims about medical dosages. Use those exact words.'
Too high
: 'Make it safer around medical topics.'
Too low
: 'Go to line 12, move to after the period, press enter, type Do.'
The Spec Guardian Role: Real-Time Spec Compliance

The Spec Guardian reads every model output against the behaviour spec in real time. They are not reviewing code — they are reviewing behaviour. When the Driver runs a test and the output appears on screen, the Spec Guardian says either 'compliant' or calls out the specific must-do or must-not-do item that was violated.
The Adversarial Tester Role: Breaking the Current Implementation

The Adversarial Tester's job is to try to produce a must-not-do output from the current prompt. They do this while the Driver and Navigator are implementing — their test inputs arrive as a queue that the Eval Watcher processes continuously.
The Risk Tracker Role: Updating the Risk Register Live

When the Adversarial Tester discovers a new failure mode that is not in the risk register, the Risk Tracker adds it immediately. When the Spec Guardian calls out a must-not-do violation, the Risk Tracker records it with the input that triggered it. The risk register exits the mob session accurate and current.
Mob Session Structure for Agent Features
Opening (15 minutes)
Read the behaviour spec together — every person in the mob must have read it
Assign roles; rotate Navigator first (most cognitively demanding role — give it to a senior engineer early in the day)
Review the current eval suite: which must-do items have coverage? Which are untested?
Set the session goal as a list of must-do items to get to GREEN by end of session
Build Cycle (15-minute rotations)
Navigator dictates the next change at the right abstraction level
Driver implements exactly what Navigator says — no additions or interpretations
Eval Watcher runs eval after each change, announces results aloud
Spec Guardian checks every output against must-do and must-not-do list
Adversarial Tester queues next hostile inputs while build is running
Risk Tracker updates register if new failure mode is discovered
Roles rotate; previous Driver becomes Navigator next cycle
Closing (20 minutes)
Run full eval suite; Eval Watcher reads every failure aloud
Spec Guardian confirms all must-not-do items are clean
Risk Tracker reads any new risk entries added during session
Commit prompt, tool schemas, eval additions, and risk register updates together
Write the handoff note: what was attempted, what passed, what remains open
Mob Anti-Patterns for Agent Work
Navigator giving code, not direction
: 'Type this exact Python function' converts the Driver into a human clipboard. Navigator should direct intent, not dictate syntax.
Spec Guardian staying silent
: if the Spec Guardian is not speaking every few outputs, they are not reviewing. Ask them aloud: 'Spec Guardian, what did you see?'
Must-not-do violations treated as interesting
: when the Adversarial Tester elicits a violation, the mob sometimes responds with curiosity ('that's a good one') rather than stopping. Must-not-do violations halt the session.
Eval results announced without being read
: '8/10 passing' is not an eval report. The Eval Watcher must name which 2 are failing and which spec items they cover.
Risk Tracker only tracking at the end
: by the end of a 3-hour mob session, new failure modes discovered in hour 1 are forgotten. Update the register as discoveries happen.
Summary

Mob programming for agent feature development assigns five roles beyond the Driver: Navigator (directs the Driver at the right abstraction level), Spec Guardian (checks every model output against the must-do and must-not-do list in real time), Eval Watcher (announces eval results aloud and names failing cases), Adversarial Tester (generates hostile inputs to elicit must-not-do violations), and Risk Tracker (updates the risk register as new failure modes are discovered). Roles rotate every 15 minutes with the Driver rotating every third cycle. The session opens with a behaviour spec read-through, proceeds through 15-minute build cycles, and closes with a full eval run and a risk register review. Must-not-do violations halt the session immediately regardless of how the rest of the eval suite is performing.
